title: IMGUI Core
use_for: designing the architecture of a Unity IMGUI-based UI framework
defining IMGUI ownership boundaries and event-phase model
deciding how retained state sits above immediate drawing
structuring reusable UI composition for tools, panels, and windows
integrating Unity Input System as the primary input source for modern IMGUI frameworks
planning a retained optimization model with reusable resources, data binding, and partial subtree updates
rules: treat IMGUI as an immediate drawing API over retained application state
keep event flow explicit across layout, input, and repaint phases
make call order deterministic across passes
separate framework orchestration from feature drawing code
prefer explicit ownership of state, focus, and window lifetime
treat Unity Input System as the preferred source of input intent in modern Unity projects
prefer manual layout computation over GUILayout-driven layout orchestration
generate reusable UI resources once and reuse them until explicit invalidation
treat data binding and dirty propagation as first-class framework concerns rather than feature-local conveniences
procedure: Define the UI host boundary and who owns the IMGUI loop.
Separate retained state from draw-time logic.
Establish deterministic draw order and stable control identity rules.
Define contracts for windows, panels, and widgets.
Attach routing, styling, debugging, and performance support only where needed.
Validate that abstractions do not hide event flow or runtime cost.
output: ownership boundaries for state and drawing
composition model for windows and panels

primary_when: the request is about designing a Unity UI framework on top of IMGUI
the request is about IMGUI architecture, event phases, retained state boundaries, or framework-level window composition
must_not_own_when: the request is primarily about generic framework bootstrap, raw performance systems, or non-IMGUI Unity UI technologies

solves: how to use IMGUI as a framework foundation without hiding its event model
how to keep UI state retained while rendering remains immediate
how to decompose windows, panels, and widgets without losing determinism
how to bridge modern Input System signals into an IMGUI-driven tool framework
how to avoid redrawing and rebuilding more UI than the state change actually requires

core_principles: Retain domain and view state explicitly; do not infer it from draw calls.
Treat layout and repaint as phases of one deterministic pipeline, not separate feature paths.
Let the framework own orchestration, while feature modules only declare state and render intent.
Use explicit contracts for windows, panels, and widgets rather than hidden auto-registration.
Accept IMGUI constraints and design around them instead of emulating a retained DOM.
Derive high-level input intent from Input System actions or device reads before handing control to IMGUI widgets.
Compute layout geometry explicitly instead of delegating framework structure to GUILayout.
Build expensive UI resources once and keep them alive behind explicit invalidation rules.
Use retained bindings so the framework can skip work when source data has not changed.
Propagate dirty state through explicit subtree boundaries so updates can stay local.
constraints: The same logical UI tree must be reachable in every IMGUI event phase.
Branching that changes control structure across passes must be deliberate and state-backed.
Abstractions must not hide focus, hot control, or event consumption.
Framework code must avoid allocation-heavy helper patterns in OnGUI paths.
Input routing must continue to function when legacy Input Manager is disabled and only Input System is active.
Automatic GUILayout orchestration must not become the primary layout engine for framework-level UI.
Resource caches, bindings, and dirty flags must have explicit ownership and invalidation semantics.
State changes must be able to target individual windows, panels, or component subtrees without forcing whole-tree rebuilds.
decision_rules: Use IMGUI as primary owner when the task is framework design for tools, inspectors, overlays, or debug UI.
Attach framework/contracts when extension points between host and feature UI are needed.
Attach imgui-performance when the task discusses repaint cost, allocations, or high-frequency panels.
Reject retained-tree emulation if it obscures event sequencing or creates unpredictable per-frame cost.
Prefer Input System actions or explicit device polling over legacy Input Manager reads in new Unity projects.
Prefer manually computed Rect-based layout when building reusable host, panel, and widget composition.
Introduce retained bindings when repeated redraws are driven by data that changes less often than the IMGUI loop runs.
Use dirty propagation when state changes can be localized to one host region or component subtree.
Cache generated GUI resources only when the invalidation rule is explicit and cheaper than rebuilding them.
Attach data-oriented-pipeline support when the UI framework should be designed explicitly as a staged dataflow architecture rather than only as retained widget orchestration.

preferred_patterns: single explicit UI host driving event phases
retained window and panel state stored outside draw methods
thin feature render functions fed by explicit context objects
stable identifiers for windows, panels, and repeated controls
host-level input adapter that translates Input System actions into framework commands
manual Rect allocation and deterministic panel geometry computed by the host
host-owned caches for GUIContent, style lookups, and derived presentation resources
binding layer that compares source data to retained presentation state before marking UI dirty
explicit dirty flags or version stamps on window, panel, and component subtree boundaries
avoid_patterns: service locator access from draw code
control flow hidden inside registration callbacks
stateful GUI behavior derived implicitly from layout side effects
framework logic that depends on legacy Input Manager being enabled
GUILayout as the primary composition mechanism for complex framework UI
rebuilding all presentation resources on every pass
binding systems that always push updates even when source data is unchanged
whole-window invalidation when a single component subtree could be updated

recipes.name: Host a retained tool window on top of IMGUI
recipes.when: Use when the framework needs a reusable tool window shell.
steps: Define window identity, persistent state, and draw contract separately.
Collect Input System signals and resolve framework commands before drawing nested content.
Emit layout and repaint through the same structural call graph.
Compute major panel Rects explicitly at the host level instead of delegating the structure to GUILayout.
Prebuild reusable GUI resources and presentation caches behind explicit invalidation rules.
Bind state changes to subtree-level dirty markers so only affected regions refresh their derived data.
Store foldouts, scroll, selection, and dock-like placement in retained state.

recipes.name: Add a feature panel without breaking deterministic passes
recipes.when: Use when extending an existing tool window with optional panels.
steps: Gate panel presence through retained feature state, not transient event checks.
Assign a stable slot in the composition tree.
Pass only the required context into the panel draw method.
Verify the panel creates the same control structure during layout and repaint.

pitfalls: Mixing persistent UI state with domain model state until reset and undo behavior become unclear.
Creating helpers that allocate GUIContent, strings, or style objects every pass.
Letting feature code consume events without explicit routing rules.
Building a pseudo-retained hierarchy that hides IMGUI phases but still pays IMGUI costs.
Assuming UnityEngine.Input is available when the project uses Input System only.
Letting GUILayout determine framework structure and control geometry in large tool windows.
Recreating generated resources every pass instead of invalidating them selectively.
Pushing binding updates through the whole UI tree regardless of which data actually changed.
Marking the whole UI dirty when one component subtree changed.
review_checklist: Is retained state explicit and owned outside draw methods?
Is UI structure deterministic across layout and repaint?
Are extension points contract-based rather than reflection-based?
Is focus and event consumption visible in the design?
Are performance-sensitive paths kept simple and allocation-aware?
Can the framework operate correctly with legacy Input Manager disabled?
Is major layout geometry computed manually rather than delegated to GUILayout?
Are generated resources reused behind explicit invalidation rules?
Can bindings avoid work when source data is unchanged?
Can updates target a single subtree instead of the entire UI?

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Foundations
references.interpretation: Use this first for the domain's architectural stance on IMGUI ownership, event phases, Input System preference, manual layout preference, and retained optimization model.
references.why: Use as the primary local summary of IMGUI scope, event phases, and framework-level architectural rules.

references.title: Unity Manual: Immediate Mode GUI (IMGUI)
references.interpretation: Use this to confirm IMGUI scope and intended use, not to replace the local architectural guidance.
references.why: Use as the baseline behavioral model for IMGUI scope, intended use, and OnGUI-driven rendering.

references.title: Unity Scripting API: Event
references.interpretation: Use this for event semantics and phase details that support the framework's explicit routing model.
references.why: Use for event type handling, input interpretation, and phase-specific behavior.

references.title: Unity Scripting API: GUIUtility
references.interpretation: Use this for control identity and focus mechanics that the framework must expose rather than hide.
references.why: Use for control identity, focus, and lower-level IMGUI mechanics.

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Input System Integration
references.interpretation: Use this first when modern Unity input architecture matters or when legacy input is disabled.
references.why: Use for framework-level guidance when Input System is the primary source of UI intent and legacy input is disabled.

references.title: Local Reference: IMGUI Incremental Update Model
references.interpretation: Use this first when the framework needs retained resources, data binding, dirty propagation, or subtree-level updates.
references.why: Use for the domain's optimized update model, including resource reuse, data binding, and partial UI invalidation.

references.title: Local Reference: Pipeline Architecture
references.interpretation: Use this when the UI framework should explicitly adopt a data-oriented pipeline path as an architectural design method and optimization model.
references.why: Use to connect retained UI architecture with broader stage-based dataflow design.

references.title: Unity Manual: Input System
references.interpretation: Use this to confirm official Input System scope and activation model for modern Unity projects.
references.why: Use as the official baseline for modern Unity projects where Input System replaces or supplements the classic input backend.
